title: Scenes From The Class Struggle In Spring

jokes.text: D'oh!
jokes.setup: Opening sequence visual gag presumably goes wrong
jokes.punchline: Homer's iconic frustrated exclamation
jokes.explanation: Iconic catchphrase, perfect character integration and economy, but predictable placement

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Ay.!: Qué agonía.!
jokes.setup: Spanish-speaking TV character expressing pain
jokes.punchline: Melodramatic Spanish exclamation about agony
jokes.explanation: Character-specific humor playing on the melodramatic nature of Spanish soap operas

dialogue.speaker: Bumblebee Man
dialogue.line: ¡Ay, ay, ay, ay! ¡El dolor! ¡El sufrimiento! ¡No puedo soportarlo!

jokes.text: Grampa destroying the TV while trying to adjust it
jokes.setup: Family trying to watch TV, Grampa wants to help with technical adjustments
jokes.punchline: Grampa's incompetent meddling destroys the television
jokes.explanation: Classic Grampa character comedy - well-structured physical gag that feels earned from his established incompetence

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Let me help you with that there television. I know all about these newfangled contraptions.

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Dad, no. Just sit down.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Nonsense! I'll just adjust this knob here...

dialogue.speaker: Marge Simpson
dialogue.line: Homer, stop him!

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: And this wire goes... somewhere important, I'm sure.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa Simpson
dialogue.line: Grandpa, you're going to break it!

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Break it? I'm fixin' it! Now where's the on switch... is it this big red button?

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Nooo!

dialogue.speaker: Bart Simpson
dialogue.line: There goes the TV.

jokes.text: No, Grampa! Don't! Dad, sit down! Gosh darn it! What does this do?
jokes.setup: Family trying to adjust the TV
jokes.punchline: Chaos as Grampa interferes with TV adjustments
jokes.explanation: Classic family chaos moment, well-executed but predictable

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, adjust the antenna!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: I'm trying, Homer!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Move it to the left!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: No, to the right!

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: You're doing it all wrong! Give it here!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Dad, you're making it worse!

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: Quiet, you! I know what I'm doing!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Now we're getting nothing but static!

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: That's called picture-in-picture, boy. Very fancy.

jokes.text: Yea! We're getting a new TV!
jokes.setup: TV just got destroyed, which should be bad news
jokes.punchline: Bart's immediate excitement about getting a new TV
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart reaction - finding opportunity in disaster, economical delivery

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Ay caramba! We can get a new TV!

jokes.text: Let's go to the Sharper Image. They've got a TV shaped like a '50s diner!
jokes.setup: Need to buy a practical new TV
jokes.punchline: Lisa suggests absurdly overdesigned luxury item from pretentious store
jokes.explanation: Sharp satire of 90s luxury retail culture, though slightly out of character for practical Lisa

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Well, if you need a practical new TV, you should check out that fancy boutique electronics store downtown. They have this amazing handcrafted, artisanal television set. It's made from reclaimed wood and features a display screen that's been individually blessed by a meditation monk.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: How much?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: No! Let's go to The Nature Company. They've got a TV assembled by Hopi Indians.
jokes.setup: Lisa suggests overpriced designer TV
jokes.punchline: Bart escalates with even more absurd 'authentic' TV option
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation that satirizes both cultural appropriation in marketing and absurd luxury positioning

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: We could get that limited edition designer TV.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? Well I want the one that's been blessed by a Tibetan monk and comes in a box made of reclaimed wood from a Victorian orphanage.

jokes.text: We can't afford to shop at any store that has a philosophy.
jokes.setup: Kids suggesting expensive boutique stores
jokes.punchline: Marge's perfectly pragmatic observation about retail philosophy and pricing
jokes.explanation: Brilliant economic observation delivered with perfect Marge pragmatism. Highly quotable insight about retail marketing that resonates beyond the show

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, I've learned that if a store is fancy enough, they don't have to charge less — they just make you feel bad about yourself until you buy something.

jokes.text: Look at these low, low prices on famous brand-name electronics!
jokes.setup: At discount electronics store
jokes.punchline: Homer's excitement about obviously knockoff products
jokes.explanation: Sets up the knockoff brand gag effectively, Homer's naivety in character

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wow! A TV that's almost as good as the real thing! And look, a Knockoff brand computer!

dialogue.speaker: Store Clerk
dialogue.line: Sir, those are clearly counterfeit.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Counterfeit? I prefer to think of them as 'inspired by' the originals. I'll take six!

jokes.text: Don't be a sap, Dad. These are just crappy knock-offs. I know a genuine Panaphonics when I see it. And look, there's Magnetbox and Sorny.
jokes.setup: Family looking at discount electronics at outlet mall
jokes.punchline: Fake brand names that are obvious knockoffs of real electronics brands
jokes.explanation: Classic Simpsons fake brand names - clever wordplay that satirizes knockoff culture with memorable made-up brands

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Look at these great deals! A Soney TV for only $49!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that says 'Soney.'

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: And check it out—a Panaphonic stereo system!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: That's not even close to Panasonic.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Who cares? And look—a Magnetbox VCR!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: It's spelled wrong on everything.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: These are the savings of a lifetime!

jokes.text: I'm not going to lie to you. Those are all superior machines. But if you like to watch your TV, and I mean really watch it...
jokes.setup: Salesman about to sell inferior TV after admitting competitors are better
jokes.punchline: Honest admission followed by transparent sales pivot
jokes.explanation: Good character work - sleazy salesman who's honest about his dishonesty, well-structured misdirection

dialogue.speaker: Salesman
dialogue.line: You're right, our competitors' TVs are better. But here's the thing—

dialogue.speaker: Salesman
dialogue.line: ours is cheaper, and that's what really matters, isn't it?

jokes.text: It features two-pronged wall plug, pre-molded hand-grip well... durable outer casing to prevent fall-apart.
jokes.setup: Salesman describing TV features
jokes.punchline: Listing basic functionality as if they're premium features
jokes.explanation: Excellent satire of marketing speak - treating basic functionality as selling points with perfectly straight delivery

dialogue.speaker: Salesman
dialogue.line: This model has a picture tube that glows when you turn it on!

dialogue.speaker: Salesman
dialogue.line: And it's got sound that comes out of these little boxes on the sides.

dialogue.speaker: Salesman
dialogue.line: You can even change the channel with this remote control — no more getting up!

dialogue.speaker: Salesman
dialogue.line: Plus, it comes in a variety of earth tones to match any living room decor.

jokes.text: Sold! You wrap it up, I'll start bringing in the pennies.
jokes.setup: Homer convinced to buy the cheap TV
jokes.punchline: Homer literally planning to pay with pennies
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer behavior - impulsive decision combined with literal interpretation of 'paying in pennies'

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A TV for $39.99? That's the best deal I've ever heard!

dialogue.speaker: Salesman
dialogue.line: Yes, sir. Will that be cash or credit?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Cash. I've got exactly 3,999 pennies in my car.

jokes.text: Honey, I don't think these clothes are us. Who are they?
jokes.setup: Marge expressing concern about clothes not fitting their identity
jokes.punchline: Homer's literal interpretation of 'these clothes are us'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer literal-mindedness turning a common expression into absurd question, excellent economy

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, these clothes don't feel like us anymore.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What are you talking about? I'm wearing them right now.

jokes.text: Hey, Brandine! You could wear this shirt to work. Oh, Cletis, you know I gotta wear the shirt what Dairy Queen give me.
jokes.setup: Rural couple shopping at outlet mall
jokes.punchline: Their simple work uniform requirements vs. fashion shopping
jokes.explanation: Good character consistency for Cletus and Brandine, showing their practical working-class priorities

dialogue.speaker: Cletus
dialogue.line: Well howdy, Brandine! Look at all these fancy clothes!

dialogue.speaker: Brandine
dialogue.line: I know, Cletus! But we ain't got money for none of this fancy stuff.

dialogue.speaker: Cletus
dialogue.line: That's the beauty of it, woman. All I need is my overalls and a t-shirt with a hole in it.

dialogue.speaker: Brandine
dialogue.line: And I just wear whatever's clean... or cleanish.

dialogue.speaker: Cletus
dialogue.line: Exactly! We're already dressed perfect for our jobs.

dialogue.speaker: Brandine
dialogue.line: We sure are, Cletus. Now let's go back to the truck and take a nap.

jokes.text: Hey, Mom.! There's something in here.!
jokes.setup: Lisa finds something in clothing
jokes.punchline: Discovery that leads to Chanel suit plot
jokes.explanation: Plot setup more than pure comedy, functional but not particularly funny

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Mom, there's a Chanel suit in here!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh my stars! Where did that come from?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: It was in the back of the closet. It still has the tag on it.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: I forgot I even had that. It must be worth a fortune!

jokes.text: Can it be a real Chanel? It is! Ninety dollars? But it's marked down from 2,800.
jokes.setup: Marge finding what appears to be designer clothing
jokes.punchline: The shocking price difference and discount psychology
jokes.explanation: Solid setup for episode's class theme, good character work showing Marge's practical mindset vs. temptation

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh my! A genuine Chanel suit for only $19.99!

dialogue.speaker: Salesman
dialogue.line: That's our clearance price, ma'am. Originally $2,000.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Two thousand dollars?! For this?

dialogue.speaker: Salesman
dialogue.line: It's 99% off. You're saving almost $2,000!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: I'm not saving money, I'm spending it! I don't have $2,000!

dialogue.speaker: Salesman
dialogue.line: But think of it as a bargain!

jokes.text: Oh, you look so sophisticated. Just like Mary Hart.
jokes.setup: Homer complimenting Marge in her new outfit
jokes.punchline: Comparing her to Entertainment Tonight host Mary Hart as the height of sophistication
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer reference point - Mary Hart represents his idea of high-class sophistication, showing his cultural limitations

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wow, Marge, you look great in that outfit! You're as sophisticated as Mary Hart from Entertainment Tonight.

jokes.text: Oh, sure I do. I treated myself to a Sanka not three days ago.
jokes.setup: Kids saying Marge never treats herself
jokes.punchline: Marge's idea of treating herself is decaffeinated coffee
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment - perfectly captures Marge's self-denial and modest expectations. Sanka as luxury is beautifully absurd

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: My idea of treating myself is decaffeinated coffee.

jokes.text: All right, I will buy it! It'll be good for the economy.
jokes.setup: Marge trying to justify buying something for herself
jokes.punchline: Rationalizing personal purchase as patriotic economic duty
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge rationalization - can't justify personal spending without making it about broader social good

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, it's actually my patriotic duty as an American to stimulate the economy. If I don't spend money, the terrorists win.

jokes.text: What about the symphony or the theater? What's the point of going out? We're just gonna wind up back here anyway.
jokes.setup: Marge wants to dress up and go somewhere nice
jokes.punchline: Homer's existential nihilism about going out
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer logic - turning romantic outing into existential observation about the futility of leaving home. Unexpectedly philosophical

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, why won't you go out somewhere nice with me?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, what's the point? We go out, we come back. We eat, we get hungry again. We go to bed, we wake up. It's all meaningless.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that's not—

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: In the end, Marge, we're all just dust returning to dust. Why dress up for dust?

jokes.text: Oh, Mrs. Simpson, you are looking very prosperous today. Might I interest you in some of our impulse items here by the cash register?
jokes.setup: Marge in her new outfit shopping at Kwik-E-Mart
jokes.punchline: Apu immediately recognizing her elevated status and targeting her for upselling
jokes.explanation: Good Apu character work - his retail instincts immediately detect higher-class customer and adjust sales approach

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: Ah, welcome back, Mrs. Simpson. I see you have upgraded your wardrobe. Please allow me to show you our premium items.

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: Perhaps some imported water? Volcanic ash? A frozen popsicle made from the tears of a lonely yak?

jokes.text: Perhaps a crazy motorized wiggle pen? Look at the craziness.
jokes.setup: Apu trying to sell impulse items
jokes.punchline: Apu's earnest description of a obviously cheap novelty pen
jokes.explanation: Apu's sincere salesmanship of garbage novelty items, 'look at the craziness' is perfect deadpan delivery

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: This pen, it is not just any pen. It is a writing instrument of distinction and sophistication. Notice how the barrel is crafted from the finest plastic, imported from... a plastic factory. And when you click it, hear that sound? That is the sound of quality. The ink, it flows like a river of words. And look here - when you turn it upside down, a tiny hula girl dances. Is this not worth the small investment of ninety-nine cents?

jokes.text: I do not speak English. But you were just talking to- Yes, yes. Hot dog, hot dog. Yes, sir. No, sir. Maybe. Okay.
jokes.setup: Customer needs gas, attendant claims not to speak English
jokes.punchline: Attendant's transparent fake non-English while clearly understanding English
jokes.explanation: Decent observational humor about service workers avoiding difficult tasks, though execution feels somewhat lazy

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: I need to fill up my tank.

dialogue.speaker: Gas Station Attendant
dialogue.line: No speaky English.

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: But you just—

dialogue.speaker: Gas Station Attendant
dialogue.line: No speaky English!

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: Fine, I'll pay with a credit card.

dialogue.speaker: Gas Station Attendant
dialogue.line: Credit card no good. Cash only.

jokes.text: Well, I can't pump it myself. I'm calling Triple 'A.'
jokes.setup: Gas station attendant won't help
jokes.punchline: Customer's helpless response to self-service
jokes.explanation: Mild satire of helpless customer behavior, but not particularly sharp or memorable

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: Help! I don't know how to use the pump!

dialogue.speaker: Gas Station Attendant
dialogue.line: It's self-service.

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: But I'm not a self!

jokes.text: And to think I heard you married Homer Simpson. I did marry Homer.
jokes.setup: Old friend Evelyn expressing surprise about Marge's marriage
jokes.punchline: Awkward beat where Marge has to confirm the socially embarrassing truth
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment - the pause and Marge's simple confirmation carries the social embarrassment perfectly

dialogue.speaker: Evelyn
dialogue.line: You married Homer Simpson? Homer Simpson?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Yes, I did.

jokes.text: We ran with different crowds. You had your debutante balls and skinny-dipping... and I had my... home shoe-repair course.
jokes.setup: Marge explaining their different high school social circles
jokes.punchline: Contrast between glamorous rich-girl activities and Marge's practical, unsexy hobby
jokes.explanation: Excellent character work - perfectly captures Marge's practical background vs. elite social world, great contrast structure

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Back in high school, I wasn't part of the popular crowd like some girls. While they were at yacht clubs and country clubs, I was at the... the DMV getting my learner's permit renewed.

jokes.text: You there. Fill it up with petroleum distillate. And revulcanize my tires, posthaste.
jokes.setup: Burns at gas station
jokes.punchline: Burns' archaic, overly formal language for basic gas station services
jokes.explanation: Classic Burns character work - treating modern gas station like he's addressing servants in 1920s, 'petroleum distillate' and 'revulcanize' are perfect

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Attendant! I require your most premium distillate. And be expeditious—I have a tee time at the club.

dialogue.speaker: Gas Station Attendant
dialogue.line: That'll be $40.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Forty dollars?! In my day, one could purchase an entire motorcar for such a sum. And the oil! Surely you have something more... refined? Perhaps imported from the Orient?

dialogue.speaker: Gas Station Attendant
dialogue.line: It's just regular unleaded, sir.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Regular? REGULAR?! I shall require your manager post-haste. This establishment is woefully inadequate.

jokes.text: Homer, I don't think you should wear a short-sleeved shirt with a tie. Oh, but Sipowicz does it.
jokes.setup: Marge giving Homer fashion advice for country club
jokes.punchline: Homer's fashion role model is a TV cop
jokes.explanation: Good Homer character work - using NYPD Blue's Sipowicz as fashion inspiration shows his working-class TV references

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you can't wear that to the country club. What's your fashion role model?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, I'd have to say Magnum P.I.

jokes.text: If Detective Sipowicz jumped off a cliff, would you do that too? Oh, I wish I was Sipowicz.
jokes.setup: Marge using classic parental argument about following others
jokes.punchline: Homer's sincere wish to be Sipowicz completely missing the point
jokes.explanation: Great subversion of classic parental lecture - Homer's earnest response completely derails Marge's logical argument

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, just because all the other fathers jump off a bridge, that doesn't mean you should too!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, if all the other fathers are doing it... Marge, I want to be Sipowicz.

jokes.text: That country club is a hotbed of exclusionist snobs and status-seeking social climbers. I've told you, I don't like you using the word 'hotbed.'
jokes.setup: Lisa giving sophisticated critique of country club culture
jokes.punchline: Marge's focus on inappropriate vocabulary rather than content
jokes.explanation: Excellent parental misdirection - Marge focusing on word propriety instead of Lisa's actual political critique

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: The country club represents an antiquated bastion of elitist exclusion, perpetuating socioeconomic stratification through artificial membership barriers.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Lisa! You can't say 'bastion' in front of the children!

jokes.text: Bart, no grifting. Aw, raspberries.
jokes.setup: Marge giving behavior instructions before country club
jokes.punchline: Bart's disappointed response to being forbidden from running scams
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart character moment - his disappointment at being forbidden from criminal activity, 'raspberries' is charmingly old-fashioned

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Now Bart, when we get to the country club, I don't want you running any of your scams.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Aw, Mom! That's like, half my identity!

jokes.text: Simpson family. We're not poor.
jokes.setup: Homer giving family name at country club entrance
jokes.punchline: Homer's unprompted defensive statement about their economic status
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer insecurity - volunteering embarrassing information unprompted, great economy in the awkward defensive statement

dialogue.speaker: Security Guard
dialogue.line: Name?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Simpson.

dialogue.speaker: Security Guard
dialogue.line: First name?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Homer.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: And before you ask, yes, we can afford to be here.

jokes.text: Well, we're not.
jokes.setup: Homer already claimed they're not poor
jokes.punchline: Homer doubling down on the embarrassing statement
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of previous joke - Homer making the situation worse by repeating himself

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: We're not poor! We're just... financially challenged.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that's the same thing.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No it's not, Marge. Poor is a state of mind. We're just temporarily broke.

jokes.text: Karyn, Gillian, Eliza-beth... Patri-cia, Robert-a, Su-san, meet Marge.
jokes.setup: Introducing wealthy friends
jokes.punchline: Pretentious pronunciation of common names
jokes.explanation: Great satire of upper-class affectation. The hyphenated pronunciations are perfectly absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Evelyn
dialogue.line: We pronounce it 'Beaumont,' not 'Bow-mont.'

dialogue.speaker: Evelyn
dialogue.line: And our son is 'Jean-Luc,' certainly not 'John-Luke.'

dialogue.speaker: Evelyn
dialogue.line: We're ever so much more refined than you common people.

jokes.text: That's the trouble with first impressions- you only get to make one.
jokes.setup: Marge's awkward introduction to the country club ladies
jokes.punchline: Woman's cutting observation delivered as fake wisdom
jokes.explanation: Sharp social observation disguised as helpful advice - perfectly captures country club passive-aggression

dialogue.speaker: Country Club Woman
dialogue.line: Well, Marge, you certainly have... bold hair. It's almost like you're trying to tell us something with that blue beehive. My therapist says that unusual hairstyles are often a cry for help. But I'm sure you're just being yourself, which is... brave.

jokes.text: Uh, come on, kids. Let's go sit in the car till your mom's done fitting in.
jokes.setup: Homer realizing they don't belong at the country club
jokes.punchline: Homer's matter-of-fact acknowledgment of their social exclusion
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer self-awareness and resignation - 'fitting in' as a process Marge needs to complete while they wait outside

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: We don't belong here.

jokes.text: I won't eat anything unless it's shipped overnight from Vermont or Washington state.
jokes.setup: Continuing pretentious food discussion
jokes.punchline: Extreme geographic food snobbery
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of food pretentiousness with specific absurd requirements

jokes.text: I get food through the mail, but in a different way. Every month, Good Housekeeping arrives in my mailbox bursting with recipes.
jokes.setup: Rich women talking about expensive mail-order foods
jokes.punchline: Marge's earnest contribution about getting food recipes through magazines
jokes.explanation: Beautiful class contrast - Marge trying to relate to elite food culture with her working-class equivalent, perfectly innocent and earnest

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, I get all my recipes from magazines.

jokes.text: Sometimes the most satisfying meal is the one you cook yourself.
jokes.setup: Marge sharing her food philosophy with rich women
jokes.punchline: Marge's genuine sentiment that completely misses their point about having servants
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge sincerity in completely wrong social context - her honest philosophy about cooking vs. their servants-dependent lifestyle

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: I always say, if you truly love your family, you have to cook for them yourself. It shows them how much you care.

dialogue.speaker: Rich Woman 1
dialogue.line: But Marge, that's what we have servants for.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh, I know. Isn't it wonderful that they have families to cook for too?

jokes.text: Instead, we microwaved our own soup! Of course it was a horrible mess. But Iris didn't mind cleaning it up.
jokes.setup: Woman sharing story of self-reliance
jokes.punchline: Her idea of independence is microwaving soup, which the maid still cleans up
jokes.explanation: Excellent satire of wealthy helplessness - treating microwaving soup as an adventure while still relying on servants for cleanup

dialogue.speaker: Country Club Woman
dialogue.line: I'm very independent. Why, just yesterday I microwaved my own soup.

dialogue.speaker: Country Club Woman
dialogue.line: Of course, my maid still had to clean up the mess.

jokes.text: I thought it was so opulent. Like the Playboy Mansion, but non-sexual.
jokes.setup: Lisa describing the country club experience
jokes.punchline: Comparing country club to Playboy Mansion minus the sexuality
jokes.explanation: Sharp cultural observation - identifying the shared aesthetic of wealth and luxury while noting the crucial difference

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: The country club is basically like the Playboy Mansion, except without all the sexuality.

jokes.text: A man in the bathroom kept handing me towels till I paid him to stop.
jokes.setup: Bart sharing his country club experience
jokes.punchline: Bart's confusion about bathroom attendant service leading to paying for it to stop
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart logic - treating bathroom attendant as annoying service to be paid to stop rather than luxury to be tipped

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, so I'm at the country club, right? And I go to the bathroom, and there's this guy in there.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: He's like, 'Let me wash your hands for you, sir.' And I'm like, 'Dude, I can wash my own hands.'

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: But he keeps following me around with paper towels and cologne and stuff. So I'm like, 'How much is this gonna cost me?'

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: And he says, 'Whatever you think is fair, sir.' So I gave him a dollar to leave me alone.

jokes.text: Shoulda held out longer, boy.
jokes.setup: Bart paid bathroom attendant to stop serving him
jokes.punchline: Homer's advice to negotiate bathroom attendant down further
jokes.explanation: Good Homer business advice in completely wrong context, treating luxury service as negotiation opportunity

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Son, if you want to negotiate with the bathroom attendant, you gotta think like a businessman. First, you lowball him. Offer him like a nickel.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: A nickel? He's asking for a quarter!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Exactly. Then when he refuses, you go up to a dime. He'll think he's winning, but you're really in control. It's called the ol' Simpson Squeeze.

jokes.text: The rich are different from you and me. Yes! They're better!
jokes.setup: Marge quoting famous literary observation about wealth
jokes.punchline: Homer's enthusiastic agreement missing the critical subtext
jokes.explanation: Excellent subversion of famous Hemingway/Fitzgerald exchange - Homer completely missing the ironic critique

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: The great Gatsby teaches us that the rich are different from you and me.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, they're better!

jokes.text: I didn't order a baloney sandwich! I ordered an abalone sandwich!
jokes.setup: Rich man at country club restaurant
jokes.punchline: Confusion between cheap lunch meat and expensive seafood
jokes.explanation: Solid wordplay joke that reinforces class themes - similar-sounding foods from opposite ends of economic spectrum

dialogue.speaker: Rich Golfer
dialogue.line: Waiter, I'll have the lobster thermidor.

dialogue.speaker: Waiter
dialogue.line: I'm afraid we're out of lobster today, sir. How about some nice bologna?

dialogue.speaker: Rich Golfer
dialogue.line: Bologna?! Do you know who I am? I don't eat lunch meat!

dialogue.speaker: Waiter
dialogue.line: Well sir, it's imported Italian bologna. Very expensive.

dialogue.speaker: Rich Golfer
dialogue.line: Imported? Well, that changes everything! I'll take it!

jokes.text: This whole country club scene is so decadent. All these spoiled brats and their smug complacent parents. It just reinforces the unspoken class system of... horses... sitting on their high... horses- I'm sorry! I gotta go!
jokes.setup: Lisa giving sophisticated political critique
jokes.punchline: Lisa's intellectual argument derailed by seeing actual horses
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment - Lisa's political sophistication completely undermined by being an eight-year-old who loves horses. Perfect structure building to the breakdown

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: The geopolitical implications of this trade policy are fundamentally flawed. We must consider the asymmetrical power dynamics and—wait, are those horses?

jokes.text: Don't ask me. I'm just hair. Your head stopped 18 inches ago.
jokes.setup: Woman making cutting remark about another's appearance
jokes.punchline: Insulting someone's hair height/volume in the cattiest possible way
jokes.explanation: Sharp insult comedy that captures country club cattiness - mathematical precision applied to personal attack

dialogue.speaker: Country club woman
dialogue.line: Nice hair. Did you get it done at the car wash?

jokes.text: I thought perhaps changing suits had gone out of fashion. Eh, Marge?
jokes.setup: Bridge game strategy discussion
jokes.punchline: Passive-aggressive comment about Marge wearing same outfit
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment showing class judgment through fashion criticism

dialogue.speaker: Country club woman
dialogue.line: Marge, dear, that's such a... unique outfit. We saw you wearing that exact dress at the club last month, didn't we?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh, well, yes, I—

dialogue.speaker: Country club woman
dialogue.line: How wonderfully practical of you. Some of us just can't help changing our clothes so frequently. Such a burden.

jokes.text: We could've stopped them if you had changed to a different suit. I thought perhaps changing suits had gone out of fashion. Eh, Marge?
jokes.setup: Women playing cards, discussing strategy
jokes.punchline: Passive-aggressive dig at Marge wearing same outfit repeatedly
jokes.explanation: Good social cruelty - using card game metaphor to attack Marge's limited wardrobe, perfectly passive-aggressive delivery

dialogue.speaker: Country club woman
dialogue.line: That's a lovely outfit, Marge. Do you wear it often?

dialogue.speaker: Other woman
dialogue.line: I've seen her in that exact same dress at least three times this month.

dialogue.speaker: Country club woman
dialogue.line: Well, some women just find what works and stick with it. How... practical.

jokes.text: That's one way. I'm P.G.A. Tour Pro Tom Kite. How about I give you a few pointers on your game?
jokes.setup: Homer cheating at golf in obvious way
jokes.punchline: Real golf pro offering to help Homer's genuinely terrible game
jokes.explanation: Standard celebrity cameo setup - real golfer helping hopeless amateur, functional but not particularly inventive

dialogue.speaker: Tom Kite
dialogue.line: Homer, I can help you with your swing. You're doing everything wrong.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Everything wrong? Tom, I've been cheating this whole time and I'm still terrible!

jokes.text: Now, you don't want to overthink. Not an issue.
jokes.setup: Golf pro giving standard advice about not overthinking
jokes.punchline: Homer's matter-of-fact acknowledgment that thinking isn't his problem
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer self-awareness delivered with complete lack of shame - economical and character-perfect

dialogue.speaker: Tom Kite
dialogue.line: Don't overthink it. Just relax and let your swing happen naturally.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Not thinking? That's my problem!

jokes.text: And stay the hell out of my locker! You can keep the shoes.
jokes.setup: Tom Kite being helpful and encouraging to Homer
jokes.punchline: Sudden shift to angry territorial behavior about his locker
jokes.explanation: Good tonal shift comedy - nice guy suddenly revealing petty territorial behavior, good contrast structure

dialogue.speaker: Tom Kite
dialogue.line: You're doing great, Homer! Keep it up!

dialogue.speaker: Tom Kite
dialogue.line: Hey! That's my locker! Get away from my locker!

jokes.text: Marge, did you know that Kennedy and Lincoln had the same handicap?
jokes.setup: Homer sharing golf trivia with Marge
jokes.punchline: Homer confusing golf handicaps with being assassinated
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy - Homer's golf obsession leading to accidentally profound observation about presidential assassinations. Perfect economy and misdirection

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Did you know that a golf handicap is when you're really bad at golf?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Yes, Homer, that's what a handicap means.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: So if someone has a handicap of 20, that means 20 assassins are after them?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: No, Homer, that's not what it means at all.

jokes.text: Marge, did you know that Kennedy and Lincoln had the same handicap?
jokes.setup: Homer sharing golf trivia
jokes.punchline: Dark double meaning about assassination vs golf scores
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark wordplay that works on multiple levels. Perfectly economical delivery.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Did you know that JFK was shot in Dallas? And his golf handicap was a 10.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: So technically, his score that day was even worse.

jokes.text: Just slap some bumper stickers on it and come to bed, will you, Marge?
jokes.setup: Marge stressed about altering her suit to look different
jokes.punchline: Homer's characteristically crude and impractical solution
jokes.explanation: Good Homer practicality - treating fashion problem like car customization, perfectly in character

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: I need to alter my suit to look different.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Just cut a hole in it and stick your belly through. Problem solved!

jokes.text: Love your outfit, Marge. The vest says, 'Let's have lunch,' but the culottes say, 'You're paying.'
jokes.setup: Woman commenting on Marge's altered outfit
jokes.punchline: Sophisticated fashion critique that's actually an insult about class
jokes.explanation: Brilliant fashion critique that's really about class warfare - perfectly captures elite passive-aggression through clothing interpretation. Extremely quotable

dialogue.speaker: Country club woman
dialogue.line: Oh, what a... charming outfit. I suppose one can find such fabrics at the... discount retailers?

jokes.text: Mom, look! I found something more fun than complaining!
jokes.setup: Lisa enjoying horseback riding at country club
jokes.punchline: Lisa's surprised discovery that she's found something better than her usual behavior
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa self-awareness - acknowledging her tendency to complain while discovering genuine joy in horses

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I can't believe it. I've finally found something I enjoy more than playing the saxophone, protesting injustice, and being intellectually superior to everyone around me.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Who knew horseback riding could be so fulfilling?

jokes.text: Where exactly will you be attaching them to that mangled Chanel suit?
jokes.setup: Marge saying she'll be there 'with bells on'
jokes.punchline: Woman's cruel observation about Marge's repeatedly altered suit
jokes.explanation: Direct cruelty masquerading as practical question - good setup with the 'bells on' idiom

dialogue.speaker: Country club woman
dialogue.line: With bells on? More like with alterations on. I've seen that suit altered more times than I've seen my own reflection.

jokes.text: Her idea of wit is nothing more than an incisive observation humorously phrased and delivered with impeccable timing.
jokes.setup: Woman defending another woman's insult about Marge
jokes.punchline: Accidentally defining exactly what good comedy writing is while dismissing it
jokes.explanation: Meta-comedy masterpiece - character accidentally providing perfect definition of comedy writing while trying to be dismissive. Extremely clever and quotable

dialogue.speaker: Country Club Woman
dialogue.line: Well, I don't think that was very nice. Making fun of someone's appearance? That's just cheap, obvious humor that anyone could write.

jokes.text: Can you bank it into the handicapped stall? Seat down.
jokes.setup: Homer practicing golf in bathroom
jokes.punchline: Treating toilet as golf target with specific requirements
jokes.explanation: Good absurd Homer behavior with specific technical golf language

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Alright, let's see... 18 holes, par 72... I'm gonna need about 50 sheets of toilet paper.

jokes.text: Homer Simpson, sir. One of the fork-and-spoon operators from Sector 7G.
jokes.setup: Burns asking about Homer's identity
jokes.punchline: Smithers' dehumanizing job description for Homer
jokes.explanation: Good Smithers corporate dehumanization - reducing Homer to utensil-operating function perfectly captures workplace hierarchy

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Smithers, who is that man?

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: That's Homer Simpson, sir. One of your organ donors—I mean, employees, in Sector 7G.

jokes.text: Except for that one in '74 when you let Richard Nixon win. That was very kind of you, sir.
jokes.setup: Smithers saying Burns never loses at golf
jokes.punchline: Revealing Burns let disgraced president win out of pity
jokes.explanation: Great historical comedy - placing Burns in position to show mercy to Nixon during Watergate scandal, perfect character moment

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Mr. Burns has never lost a game of golf in his life.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What about that time he played against the former president?

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Oh, he let him win out of pity.

jokes.text: Oh, he just looked so forlorn, Smithers, with his 'I can't go to prison, Monty. They'll eat me alive!'
jokes.setup: Burns explaining why he let Nixon win
jokes.punchline: Burns doing Nixon impression focused on prison fears
jokes.explanation: Excellent historical comedy - Burns' Nixon impression captures both the political moment and Burns' condescending superiority

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: I let Nixon win because a man in his position needs a certain... flexibility with the law.

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Besides, I admired his work. *does Nixon impression* I'm not a crook! Well, not in the way they mean anyway. Say, what's the minimum security prison like these days? Do they have golf?

jokes.text: Oh, he just looked so forlorn, Smithers, with his... 'I can't go to prison, Monty. They'll eat me alive!'
jokes.setup: Burns explaining why he let Nixon win
jokes.punchline: Nixon's pathetic plea about prison vulnerability
jokes.explanation: Good Burns impression of Nixon with specific vulnerability fears

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: I could have had Nixon killed, but I decided to let him win. You see, I was moved by his pathetic plea about how vulnerable he'd be in prison.

jokes.text: Say, I wonder if this Homer Nixon is any relation?
jokes.setup: Burns hearing about Homer Simpson
jokes.punchline: Burns confusing Homer's last name with Nixon
jokes.explanation: Good Burns confusion comedy - his age and disconnect from reality leading to name confusion

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Simpson, eh? Like Nixon?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No, Simpson.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Yes, yes, Simpson. Close enough.

jokes.text: Unlikely, sir. They-They spell and pronounce their name differently.
jokes.setup: Burns asking if Homer Simpson is related to Nixon
jokes.punchline: Smithers' overly technical explanation of obvious difference
jokes.explanation: Perfect Smithers bureaucratic precision applied to obvious situation - treating completely different names as technical distinction

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Smithers, is Homer Simpson any relation to Richard Nixon?

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: No sir, Mr. Burns. Homer Simpson is a nuclear safety inspector at the Springfield Nuclear Power Plant, whereas former President Nixon was a politician who served from 1969 to 1974 and resigned due to the Watergate scandal. Additionally, Mr. Simpson has only two eyes, while Nixon also had two eyes, though their facial structures, genealogies, and career trajectories are entirely distinct.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Ah yes, quite right Smithers. Carry on.

jokes.text: Whee! Whee! Mom! Mom! Mom! Mom! Mom! Lisa, please!
jokes.setup: Marge trying to concentrate on altering her suit
jokes.punchline: Kids' increasing demands overwhelming Marge's attempts to focus
jokes.explanation: Standard family chaos escalation - kids interrupting parent at worst moment, functional but not particularly innovative

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Mom, can you help me with my homework?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Not right now, sweetie. Mommy's trying to concentrate.

dialogue.speaker: Maggie
dialogue.line: *sucks pacifier loudly*

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: But Mom, it's really important!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Lisa, please, just five more minutes.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Hey Mom, where's my clean underwear?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge! Where's my dinner?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Everyone just... QUIET! I'm trying to alter this suit!

jokes.text: At times like this I guess all you can do is laugh.
jokes.setup: Marge's suit completely destroyed
jokes.punchline: Marge's forced optimism in face of disaster, followed by awkward silence
jokes.explanation: Good dark comedy moment - Marge's attempt at positive thinking immediately undermined by reality, silence sells it

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, I suppose there's a bright side to everything. At least now I have an excuse to go shopping for a new outfit!

jokes.text: At times like this I guess all you can do is laugh.
jokes.setup: Marge's suit completely ruined
jokes.punchline: Forced optimism in crisis moment
jokes.explanation: Good Marge moment trying to stay positive in disaster

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, I suppose it could be worse. At least my hair is still perfect!

jokes.text: I need a formal dress for tonight! You've come to the right place. We've got classy duds up the ying-yang.
jokes.setup: Marge needing formal wear at discount store
jokes.punchline: Clerk's crude expression for having 'classy' items
jokes.explanation: Good contradiction - claiming classiness while using crude expression

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Do you have any formal wear?

dialogue.speaker: Thrift Store Clerk
dialogue.line: Yeah, we got some classy stuff. Just came in from an estate sale.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh wonderful!

dialogue.speaker: Thrift Store Clerk
dialogue.line: Yeah, we're really moving up in the world. Next thing you know, we'll have a customer with all their original teeth.

jokes.text: It's got some cigarette burns, but we can patch them up with Nu Vinyl.
jokes.setup: Clerk showing Marge formal dress options
jokes.punchline: Describing damage and terrible repair solution as selling points
jokes.explanation: Standard discount store humor - presenting defects as fixable with obviously inappropriate materials

dialogue.speaker: Discount Store Clerk
dialogue.line: This one here has a stain, but if you turn it inside out, nobody will notice. And this beauty has a broken zipper, but that just means it's got character. As for this dress, well, the hem is coming undone, but I've got some duct tape in the back that'll fix it right up!

jokes.text: This was originally a Halloween costume... but it found its way into my regular rotation.
jokes.setup: Clerk showing another dress option
jokes.punchline: Clerk's personal admission about wearing Halloween costume as regular clothing
jokes.explanation: Good character work - clerk's inappropriate personal sharing while trying to make sale, reveals questionable fashion choices

dialogue.speaker: Discount Store Clerk
dialogue.line: Here's another option.

dialogue.speaker: Discount Store Clerk
dialogue.line: I've been wearing this Halloween costume since 1997.

jokes.text: This was originally a Halloween costume... but it found its way into my regular rotation.
jokes.setup: Showing another dress option
jokes.punchline: Casual admission of wearing costume as regular clothes
jokes.explanation: Good character detail showing economic desperation with casual delivery

dialogue.speaker: Salesperson
dialogue.line: How about this one?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh, it's lovely!

dialogue.speaker: Salesperson
dialogue.line: Yes, I wear it every day.

jokes.text: Rant on, Simpson, but your vainglorious boasting... will only add savor to my inevitable triumph.
jokes.setup: Burns responding to Homer's golf confidence
jokes.punchline: Overly formal threat with archaic language
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns verbosity and archaic vocabulary use

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Simpson, your audacity is only matched by your incompetence. I shall take great pleasure in firing you... personally. Consider yourself on notice that your employment hangs by a thread as gossamer as a spider's web in a hurricane.

jokes.text: Yes. You're in deep 'd'oh' now.
jokes.setup: Homer expressing frustration during golf game
jokes.punchline: Burns making pun on Homer's catchphrase
jokes.explanation: Competent but predictable pun on Homer's signature exclamation - functional callback but not particularly clever

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: That's right, Simpson. D'oh is right.

jokes.text: Oh, quit cogitating, Steinmetz, and use an open-faced club- the sand wedge!
jokes.setup: Burns giving Homer golf advice
jokes.punchline: Burns' overly intellectual approach to simple golf advice
jokes.explanation: Good Burns character work - using 'cogitating' and calling Homer 'Steinmetz' shows his pompous intellectual pretensions

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Simpson, you must approach this golf swing with the precision of a Swiss chronometer and the grace of a Balanchine dancer. The golf ball is but a metaphor for life itself — spherical, insignificant, and destined to disappear into a hole.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: So... just hit the ball?

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Good heavens, no! You must first meditate upon the cosmic significance of your stance, contemplate the Newtonian physics governing trajectory, and achieve a state of Zen-like oneness with your nine iron.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'll just swing real hard.

jokes.text: No, but we do have a shipment of slightly burnt Sears activewear coming in this afternoon.
jokes.setup: Marge desperately asking about high-quality clothes
jokes.punchline: Store's best offer is damaged discount athletic wear
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Marge's desperation - store offering increasingly inappropriate alternatives to formal wear

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Do you have any high-quality clothes?

dialogue.speaker: Store clerk
dialogue.line: Our best offer is damaged discount athletic wear.

jokes.text: Oh, I give up. What time, and how burnt?
jokes.setup: Store offering damaged athletic wear
jokes.punchline: Marge's complete resignation leading to practical questions about terrible option
jokes.explanation: Excellent desperation comedy - Marge's standards completely collapsing into practical questions about obviously terrible merchandise

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, I suppose if the store is only selling damaged athletic wear, we don't really have much choice.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: But I do have a practical question: how damaged are we talking here?

jokes.text: Uh, there are no other balls. Just these, uh, reptile eggs! Oh, step away! They're endangered.
jokes.setup: Homer discovering Smithers' golf ball cheating scheme
jokes.punchline: Smithers' obviously fake excuse about reptile eggs
jokes.explanation: Good Smithers panic comedy - transparent lie made worse by adding environmental protection angle

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait a minute... these golf balls are filled with something!

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Oh, Mr. Homer, those aren't golf balls. They're... they're reptile eggs. Yes, rare reptile eggs I'm incubating for the Springfield Zoo.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Reptile eggs? In a bucket of sand in the country club?

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: The sand maintains optimal temperature and humidity, sir. It's all very scientific.

jokes.text: Good lord, Smithers! That's patently unnecessary. I'm one of the world's finest golfers.
jokes.setup: Burns discovering Smithers' cheating on his behalf
jokes.punchline: Burns' complete delusion about his golf abilities
jokes.explanation: Good Burns delusion about his abilities despite years of cheating

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Smithers, you've been cheating on my behalf? How dare you! I'll have you know my golf score of 18 is completely legitimate.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Sir, that's 18 over par...

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Nonsense! I'm a natural. Why, I once shot a 12 on a par 5. The ball simply obeyed my superior intellect and flew directly into the cup.

jokes.text: Wait till I tell everyone about this. You stink.
jokes.setup: Homer discovering Burns cheats at golf
jokes.punchline: Childish threat and insult
jokes.explanation: Good Homer childishness but not particularly clever

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You cheated at golf, Mr. Burns! That's... that's the worst thing you've ever done!

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Simpson, I've done far worse things.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, I'm gonna tell everyone you're a big stupid head!

jokes.text: Bull hockey! I don't care about joining this stupid club. But does your wife?
jokes.setup: Homer refusing to keep quiet about Burns' cheating
jokes.punchline: Smithers using Marge's dreams against Homer's principles
jokes.explanation: Good moral dilemma comedy - Homer's integrity vs. Marge's happiness, Smithers finding the perfect pressure point

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I don't care what Mr. Burns wants! I'm going to tell everyone he cheated!

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Homer, what if I told you that Marge has been having those dreams about you again?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What? Really? Which ones?

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: The kind where you're... attentive and helpful around the house.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: My lips are sealed!

jokes.text: I knew my kind wasn't welcome here.
jokes.setup: Homer realizing he's being blackmailed to stay quiet
jokes.punchline: Homer's melodramatic interpretation of class discrimination
jokes.explanation: Good Homer victimization comedy - turning personal moral dilemma into class warfare narrative

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: They're blackmailing me to stay quiet? This is discrimination! The rich can blackmail people and nobody cares, but the moment a working man tries to blackmail somebody, he's a criminal!

jokes.text: The outlet store. Wow! Two finds in one store. What are the odds? Call it fate. Let's go!
jokes.setup: Lisa asking where the dress came from
jokes.punchline: Marge's increasingly desperate evasions
jokes.explanation: Good escalating panic from Marge as Lisa gets suspicious

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Where did you get that dress, Mom?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh, this old thing? I've had it for years.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: But Mom, I saw the price tag still on it.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, I... I was saving it for a special occasion!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Mom, it has today's date on it.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Today IS special! Look, a cloud that looks like a duck!

jokes.text: Wow! Two finds in one store. What are the odds?
jokes.setup: Marge claiming both suits came from outlet store
jokes.punchline: Lisa's increasingly suspicious mathematical observation
jokes.explanation: Good Lisa skepticism - her logical mind questioning Marge's obviously false story about finding two designer dresses

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Wait a minute. Mom, if both suits came from the same outlet store, how is it possible that one is polyester and the other is cotton? That would violate the fundamental principles of inventory management and supply chain logistics.

jokes.text: For once maybe someone will call me 'sir' without adding, 'You're making a scene.'
jokes.setup: Homer excited about valet parking
jokes.punchline: Homer's awareness of his typical public behavior problems
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer self-awareness - revealing he's conscious of his inappropriate public behavior while hoping for different treatment. Perfect character insight

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Valet parking! Finally, a chance to make a good first impression.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, they're not going to let me back in, are they?

jokes.text: I'm gonna pose as an Italian count... and get some old lady to leave me all her money.
jokes.setup: Bart planning country club activities
jokes.punchline: Elaborate inheritance con scheme
jokes.explanation: Good Bart criminal ambition with specific elaborate scheme

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: So I'm thinking we could do some golfing, maybe hit the links at the country club...

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Country club? Bart, we can't afford that!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Relax, Dad. I've got it all figured out. We just need to find some rich old guy, get him to like us, and then...

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Bart, what are you saying?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I'm saying we butter him up, become his best friends, convince him we're the perfect family, and when he kicks the bucket—boom—we inherit everything!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Now that's the kind of planning I can get behind!

jokes.text: I'm gonna ask people if they know their servants' last names. Or, in the case of butlers, their first.
jokes.setup: Lisa planning mischief at the country club ball
jokes.punchline: Lisa's sophisticated critique of master-servant relationships disguised as innocent questions
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Lisa social commentary - exposing class hierarchy through deceptively innocent questions. Shows sophisticated understanding of power dynamics

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: So tell me, Daddy, in a master-servant relationship, doesn't the servant become dependent on the master for their sense of self-worth?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Uh, sure, sweetie.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: And doesn't that dependency prevent them from ever achieving true autonomy or happiness?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, yeah, whatever you say.

jokes.text: Now that she's a better person... we can see how awful we really are.
jokes.setup: Homer reflecting on Marge's transformation
jokes.punchline: Homer's accidentally profound observation about relative moral improvement
jokes.explanation: Unexpectedly deep Homer philosophy - genuine insight about how one person's growth can highlight others' flaws

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know, Marge used to be a real goody-two-shoes. But now she's committed all these crimes. I guess compared to her, I'm actually the moral one in this relationship.

jokes.text: I like Bart.
jokes.setup: Marge listing what she likes about each family member
jokes.punchline: Marge's struggle to find specific positive qualities about Bart
jokes.explanation: Perfect parental honesty - Marge's pause and simple statement revealing the challenge of describing Bart positively

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer is big and strong. Lisa is smart and talented. Maggie is cute and cuddly. But Bart... Bart is...

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, he's... he's got a certain... he's very... you know, Bart is...

jokes.text: Our savings? Don't worry. I saved the receipt. We'll have a $3,300 credit at Chanel. They have beer and gum, right?
jokes.setup: Homer learning about expensive dress purchase
jokes.punchline: Assumption that luxury store sells convenience items
jokes.explanation: Good Homer misunderstanding of luxury retail with specific humble needs

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, you spent $3,000 on a dress?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: It's a designer dress, Homer.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, did it come with a Big Gulp?

jokes.text: Our savings? Don't worry. I saved the receipt. We'll have a $3,300 credit at Chanel. They have beer and gum, right?
jokes.setup: Homer learning Marge spent their savings on dress
jokes.punchline: Homer's assumption that luxury boutique sells convenience store items
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer class confusion - treating Chanel like a corner store, revealing his complete disconnect from luxury retail

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A boutique?! What kind of store is that?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: It's a fancy clothing store, Homer.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Fancy? Do they have Squishees?

jokes.text: I wouldn't want to join any club that would have this me as a member.
jokes.setup: Family leaving country club
jokes.punchline: Marge adapting Groucho Marx quote with grammatical error
jokes.explanation: Good cultural reference adaptation - Marge's attempt at sophisticated quote undermined by adding 'this' before 'me'

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: I would never want to be a member of a club that would have someone like me as a member.

jokes.text: I hope she didn't take my attempt to destroy her too seriously.
jokes.setup: Woman wondering why Marge didn't show up for initiation
jokes.punchline: Woman's casual admission of deliberate psychological cruelty
jokes.explanation: Excellent villain comedy - woman's casual admission of deliberate cruelty delivered with complete lack of self-awareness

dialogue.speaker: Country Club Woman
dialogue.line: Marge was supposed to be here for her initiation. I wonder where she is.

dialogue.speaker: Country Club Woman
dialogue.line: Oh well, we told her the initiation was today when it's actually next week. We do that to all the new members. It really messes with their heads.

jokes.text: Mmm! I- Ugh!
jokes.setup: Burns trying cake that Smithers made for Homer
jokes.punchline: Burns' immediate disgust at Smithers' cooking
jokes.explanation: Standard physical comedy reaction - Burns tasting bad food, functional but not particularly memorable

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: What is this slop, Smithers? It tastes like a tire fire mixed with a urinal cake.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Sir, I followed the recipe exactly.

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Well, your exact is execrable. Get this swill away from me before I have you executed.

jokes.text: I pickled the figs myself.
jokes.setup: Burns disgusted by cake
jokes.punchline: Smithers' proud explanation of disgusting ingredient choice
jokes.explanation: Good Smithers character work - his pride in terrible culinary choice explaining Burns' reaction

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Smithers, this cake is revolting. What on earth did you put in it?

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Well sir, I thought you'd appreciate a cake made with the finest ingredients. So I used only the most expensive imported butter, Belgian chocolate, and caviar.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Caviar? In a cake?

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Yes sir. I was quite proud of that touch.

jokes.text: But you know, we realized we're more comfortable in a place like this.
jokes.setup: Family back at working-class restaurant
jokes.punchline: Marge's acceptance of their true social class
jokes.explanation: Good character resolution - Marge finding peace with their actual social position rather than pretending to be something else

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, I suppose this is where we belong. At least the meatloaf is affordable.

jokes.text: Man, you're crazy! This place is a dump. Oh, man! I'd be anywhere except this place. That's for sure.
jokes.setup: Marge expressing contentment with their regular restaurant
jokes.punchline: Other customer's honest assessment of the same place
jokes.explanation: Good ironic ending - undermining Marge's newfound contentment with objective reality about their restaurant choice

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: I just love this restaurant. The owner knows us by name, the waiters treat us like family, and the food is always fresh and delicious.

dialogue.speaker: Restaurant Customer
dialogue.line: Yeah, that's because nobody else comes here.

tmdb_overview: A chance encounter with a former classmate yields an invitation to the Springfield Country Club, where Marge tries to parlay a bargain basement Chanel suit into elevated social status for her family.
insight: Class anxiety drives 99 jokes across 22 minutes at steady 4.9-per-minute pace.
